content:
> I tried every solutions above but still failed to fix it. However after cleaning `Derived Data` in Xcode, it magically worked...
> In `XCode -> Preference -> Locations -> Derived Data`, click the little arrow to open the path in finder and clean the folder. Then `flutter run` resume working normally.

Dude, you rock!

This issue has completely run away with "me too"s, so I'll try cleaning it up a bit.

1. The flood of reports of this issue coincided with the iOS 14.4.* updates.  Long app launch times is a commonly reported problem when there's a pending iOS update on your test device, or when your test device is running a newer OS than the one shipped with Xcode.  It's also commonly reported right after you upgrade your test OS because Xcode can get stuck trying to copy the developer image over to the device.  Many of these reports are that it works on one device and not on another device running the same OS.  Frankly there's not much Flutter can do to work around this when the problem is between the test device and Xcode.  
1. Make sure Xcode is updated and your iOS device is updated.  You can also try opening `ios/Runner.xcworkspace` in Xcode and running your app from there to unwedge it.  
1. You can also try running `flutter clean` to clean Xcode's build folder (DerivedData).
1. If you "fixed" this by editing the Flutter SDK app launch timeout (which at 30 seconds is already very generous if it wasn't for the iOS/Xcode issue), https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/81229 PR will resolve your issue so you don't need to do this manually.
1. Additionally @stx notes switching which port your device is tethered to can make the app launch much faster.
> I disconnected and reconnected the same USB-C to Lightning cable to the same port on a MBP and rebuilt over and over with the same observatory error, but the moment I changed to a different port, debugging succeeded because the install/launch process completed much more quickly.
> 
> Either way, I would strongly recommend trying other ports.

6. It seems like a cop-out, but swapping your cable may also help.

> Funny, when the app launched again i got printed [this](https://flutter.dev/docs/development/add-to-app/ios/project-setup#local-network-privacy-permissions) Link, that no one from the Flutter/Google Team posted before. After Reading it, i did not had the _dartobservatory._tcp value added in the info.plist, that may have been the Problem.

Those instructions are for add-to-app.  You don't need to manually edit anything if you are running from a `flutter create`d app.

This worked for me.
Open `ios/Runner.xcworkspace` in Xcode.
Xcode should detect that it needs to download some updates.
After downloading everything works normally.
Device: iPhone 11 iOS 14.5.1
MacOS : 11.3.1
Xcode: 12.5

I had the same problem, but once I initialized the MAC and built it again, the MAC got angry with the iproxy permissions.
(MacOS: BigSur 11.4)
The following actions are said to be deprecated, but may be buildable.

$ cd ~/development/flutter/bin/cache/artifacts/libimobiledevice
$ sudo xattr -d com.apple.quarantine idevicesyslog
$ cd ~/development/flutter/bin/cache/artifacts/usbmuxd
$ sudo xattr -d com.apple.quarantine iproxy

I hope this helps a little.

Anyone has a fix now? It is happening on my iPhone 12 Pro Max and iPad 8th Generation but on my iPad Pro it works.

There should probably be a more general issue opened. I'm 100% unable to build on my iPhone now for any project. A freshly created project with no dependencies will not build because of the waiting for observatory port timeout.

Looking at the code the only reasonable change I can think of is increasing the timeout. But this seems hacky. Have not tested this yet.

Hey guys I have the solution!!

I cannot say if the issue is on the side of Xcode for the remote package timeout or if its our phones. I tested ALL Solutions every related to these threads and had no success. Now I found a workaround:

Step 1: Disconnect your Device
Step 2: Close Xcode
Step 3: In the Developer Settings of you Device -> Clear trusted Computers
Step 4: Delete all Apps you ever debugged on your Device 

Step 5: In  
``` 
/Library/Developer/Xcode/iOS\ DeviceSupport 
```
-> delete the Folder your IOS Version of your device, mine was 14.6

Step 6: Create in your home directory a file called 
```.lldbinit``` 
and enter this code:
```
settings set plugin.process.gdb-remote.packet-timeout 300
```

Step 7:
Open the Flutter SDK File 
```
/flutter/packages/flutter_tools/lib/src/ios/devices.dart 
```

Edit this Code to:

From 
```
localUri = await observatoryDiscovery.uri.timeout(const Duration(seconds: 30));
```
To:
```
localUri = await observatoryDiscovery.uri.timeout(const Duration(seconds: 300));
```

Edit this Code in the same file from:
```
static const Duration _kiProxyPortForwardTimeout = Duration(seconds: 1);
```

To:

```
static const Duration _kiProxyPortForwardTimeout = Duration(seconds: 5);
```

Delete then this file to rebuild the Flutter Tools later:

```
flutter/bin/cache/flutter_tools.snapshot
```
and 

```
flutter/bin/cache/flutter_tools.stamp
```

Step 8:

Open Xcode

Step 9:

Connect your Device, a new folder in with your Device Version which we deleted once will be created in:
```
/Library/Developer/Xcode/iOS\ DeviceSupport 
```


Step 10:

Run your App from Flutter (not necessary to run it from Xcode!) (also run in verbose to see the same logs as me now)

You will see when its coming to:
```
Application launched on the device. Waiting for observatory port
```

These Logs appears:
```
(lldb) 2021-07-17 17:14:44.974661+0200 Runner[7493:2479838] fopen
failed for data file: errno = 2 (No such file or directory)
[   +1 ms] Errors found! Invalidating cache...

Then it works!!!

For the Flutter Team:

I don't know, if the issue is with the iProxy Timeout, the Remote packages from Xcode Timeout or just the IOS Device Support.

Reposting this since it was buried in the "me too" comments.  Unfortunately GitHub doesn't let us pin comments.  Note #81229 fix referenced is available in 2.3.0-1.0.pre which is [currently available](https://flutter.dev/docs/development/tools/sdk/releases?tab=macos) on the [beta channel](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/wiki/Flutter-build-release-channels#how-to-change-channels).

> 1. The flood of reports of this issue coincided with the iOS 14.4.* updates.  Long app launch times is a commonly reported problem when there's a pending iOS update on your test device, or when your test device is running a newer OS than the one shipped with Xcode.  It's also commonly reported right after you upgrade your test OS because Xcode can get stuck trying to copy the developer image over to the device.  Many of these reports are that it works on one device and not on another device running the same OS.  Frankly there's not much Flutter can do to work around this when the problem is between the test device and Xcode.
> 2. Make sure Xcode is updated and your iOS device is updated.  You can also try opening `ios/Runner.xcworkspace` in Xcode and running your app from there to unwedge it.
> 3. You can also try running `flutter clean` to clean Xcode's build folder (DerivedData).
> 4. If you "fixed" this by editing the Flutter SDK app launch timeout (which at 30 seconds is already very generous if it wasn't for the iOS/Xcode issue), [[flutter_tools] remove timeout from iOS device startup #81229](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/81229) PR will resolve your issue so you don't need to do this manually.
> 5. Additionally @stx notes switching which port your device is tethered to can make the app launch much faster.
> 
> > I disconnected and reconnected the same USB-C to Lightning cable to the same port on a MBP and rebuilt over and over with the same observatory error, but the moment I changed to a different port, debugging succeeded because the install/launch process completed much more quickly.
> > Either way, I would strongly recommend trying other ports.
> 
> 6. It seems like a cop-out, but swapping your cable may also help.
Just updated my iPhone 12 mini to iOS 14.7 and it started working again. I also have that timeout fix added to flutter. I will update if it stops working.

Also having random devices that don't work.
But managed to get it working with these steps:

* follow https://stackoverflow.com/a/40327522/2553215 and clear all XCode caches, data, DeviceSupport and so on
* run `flutter clean`
* on the device go to developer tools and "Clear Trusted Computers"
* re-connect device and try again
* a dialog should show up "Do you want to allow Flutter Tools to....." -> accept it

> * re-connect device and try again
> * a dialog should show up "Do you want to allow Flutter Tools to....." -> accept it

I got the same issue! **Build Device**: 16 inch MacBook Pro (BigSur 11.5.2) **Physical Device**: iPhone 12 Pro Max (iOS 14.7.1) ## Issue - `flutter run` / (Clicking the ▶️ in Android Studio) takes too long to attach the debugging port until eventually timeout and the app crashed. - It worked perfectly fine if we run it directly from **Xcode** (12.5.1) - It worked perfectly fine with iPhone 7s (iOS 14.7.1) - It worked perfectly fine with iPhone 11 Pro Max (iOS 14.7.1) - Also worked fine on any device if I use `flutter run --release` ## Workaround 1. Open the iOS project in Xcode. 2. Click run normally and wait until the app successfully launched and do not close the app. 3. Open the terminal inside your Flutter project. 4. Run command `flutter attach --app-id com.example.myapp` (replace `com.example.myapp` with your app bundle ID) 5. Done. However, you may need to manually reload the app by pressing `r` ## Information

Reposting this again since it was buried in the "me too" comments.  Unfortunately GitHub doesn't let us pin comments, and I don't want to lock this issue.  PLEASE do not comment on this issue to suggest the same thing as this list, or to say one of them worked.  You're just adding noise to this issue and pushing up actually helpful suggestions.

#81229 is available in Flutter 2.5, so you may see "iOS Observatory not discovered after 30 seconds. This is taking much longer than expected..." but it won't actually time out.  Upgrading Flutter to 2.5 or higher should be the first thing you try when you see this issue.

1. The flood of reports of this issue coincided with an iOS update.  Long app launch times is a commonly reported problem when there's a pending iOS update on your test device, or when your test device is running a newer OS than the one shipped with Xcode.  It's also commonly reported right after you upgrade your test OS because Xcode can get stuck trying to copy the developer image over to the device.  Many of these reports are that it works on one device and not on another device running the same OS.  Frankly there's not much Flutter can do to work around this when the problem is between the test device and Xcode.
2. Make sure Xcode is updated and your iOS device is updated.  You can also try opening `ios/Runner.xcworkspace` in Xcode and running your app from there to unwedge it.
3. You can also try running `flutter clean` to clean Xcode's build folder (DerivedData).
4. Additionally switching which port your device is tethered to can make the app launch much faster.  It seems like a cop-out, but swapping your cable may also help.
5. Unpair the device and re-pair it by navigating on the device to Settings > Developer > "Clear Trusted Computers", then re-pair your device.

To reiterate, PLEASE do not add a comment unless it's something different from what's has already been said in this issue.

@jmagman, THANKS!
 I tried all the methods described above, but the only thing that solved this issue:

>You can also try opening ios/Runner.xcworkspace in Xcode and running your app from there to unwedge it.
